synopsis: Rework challenges conventional business wisdom by arguing that plans, investors, large teams, and physical offices are unnecessary obstacles rather than requirements for success. The book offers direct, actionable advice for entrepreneurs and small-business owners on building profitable companies through simplicity, focus, and getting things done.

sources.title: Rework by Jason Fried and David Heinemeier Hansson | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Drawing on more than twenty-five years of interviews with prominent investors including Charlie Munger, John Templeton, and Jack Bogle, William Green examines the mental models and decision-making habits that drive sustained long-term investment success. The book argues that the same principles of rational, disciplined thinking that produce market outperformance also apply to building a better life.

Ringworld follows four mismatched travelers—a bored human, a cowardly alien diplomat, a young woman, and a kzin warrior—dispatched to explore an enormous artificial ring structure orbiting a distant star with three million times Earth's surface area. Their expedition collapses when their ship crashes, stranding the crew on a perilous trek across the vast alien construct.

sources.title: Ringworld by Larry Niven | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Ripples in Spacetime traces the decades-long international effort to detect gravitational waves, explaining the physics of Einstein's prediction and the precision engineering of the LIGO detectors that finally confirmed it in 2015. The book examines how this discovery launched a new field of gravitational-wave astronomy capable of observing black hole collisions and neutron star mergers invisible to conventional telescopes.

sources.title: Ripples in Spacetime — Harvard University Press

synopsis: Running on Empty recounts ultramarathoner Marshall Ulrich's attempt to set a record running across America from California to New York—the equivalent of two marathons and a 10K every day for nearly two months. Woven throughout the account of physical punishment is the story of how the death of his first wife decades earlier drove him to extreme endurance running as a response to grief.

sources.title: Running on Empty by Marshall Ulrich | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Sandworm investigates Sandworm, an elite Russian hacking unit responsible for some of the most destructive cyberattacks in history, including the NotPetya malware that caused billions in global damage and repeated strikes on Ukraine's power grid. The book argues that these operations represent a new era of warfare in which the boundary between peacetime sabotage and armed conflict has effectively dissolved.

sources.title: Sandworm by Andy Greenberg | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Saving Capitalism argues that the concept of a free market is a myth, because markets are human constructs whose rules are continuously shaped by the political power of corporations, wealthy individuals, and Wall Street at the expense of the middle class. Robert Reich proposes institutional reforms to rewrite the rules of the market so that prosperity is distributed broadly rather than concentrated at the top.

sources.title: Saving Capitalism by Robert B. Reich | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Scale presents a unified mathematical framework showing that organisms, cities, companies, and economies all obey the same power-law scaling rules, where metabolic rates, innovation output, and income scale predictably with size. Physicist Geoffrey West uses these quantitative patterns to explore questions about growth limits, the pace of urban life, and why companies—unlike cities—almost inevitably stagnate and die.

sources.title: Scale by Geoffrey West | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Sea of Tranquility spans six centuries—from 1912 Vancouver Island to a lunar colony in the far future—connecting characters whose lives intersect through a mysterious temporal anomaly. When a detective in Night City investigates the glitch, his inquiry links an exiled aristocrat, a novelist writing during a pandemic, and a childhood friend whose choice threatens to unravel history.

sources.title: Sea of Tranquility by Emily St. John Mandel | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Second Foundation concludes the original Foundation trilogy with two interleaved stories: the First Foundation's desperate search for the hidden Second Foundation, and the Second Foundation's covert efforts to steer galactic history back onto Hari Seldon's planned course after the mutant Mule's conquest. Asimov uses the conflict to explore themes of free will, mental power, and whether psychohistory's blueprint for civilization can survive its own exposure.

sources.title: Second Foundation by Isaac Asimov | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Secrets of Professional Turf Betting presents the methods and strategies used by professional horse-race bettors to generate consistent profits, covering topics from handicapping techniques and price-line construction to bankroll management and identifying overlay plays. Robert Bacon draws on extensive firsthand track experience across North America to distinguish disciplined professional wagering from the haphazard approach of recreational gamblers.

sources.title: Secrets of Professional Turf Betting - Google Books

synopsis: Secrets of Sand Hill Road provides an insider's guide to how venture capital funds raise money, evaluate startups, structure term sheets, and manage portfolio companies, written by the managing partner of Andreessen Horowitz. The book explains the incentives and decision-making logic of VCs so that founders can negotiate more effectively and build productive long-term relationships with their investors.

sources.title: Secrets of Sand Hill Road by Scott Kupor | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Security Analysis, first published in 1934 by Benjamin Graham and David Dodd, establishes the principles of value investing, arguing that careful examination of a company's balance sheet and earnings power—not market sentiment—is the primary basis for sound investment decisions. The text introduces the margin of safety concept and provides systematic techniques for distinguishing investment from speculation.

See You in the Cosmos follows eleven-year-old Alex Petroski, who records his observations of life on Earth onto a gold iPod he plans to launch into space on a homemade rocket, inspired by Carl Sagan's Voyager recordings. His journey to a rocketry festival leads him across the country, where he encounters a range of strangers who gradually reveal hidden truths about his family.

sources.title: See You in the Cosmos by Jack Cheng | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Seeing Like a State examines why large-scale state schemes to improve human welfare—from Soviet collectivization to Le Corbusier's urban planning—repeatedly cause catastrophic harm when governments impose simplified order on complex social systems they cannot fully understand. James C. Scott argues that local, practical knowledge is irreplaceable and that high-modernist confidence in central planning undermines the very conditions its success depends on.

sources.title: Seeing Like a State | Yale University Press

synopsis: Seeking Wisdom synthesizes insights from evolutionary biology, psychology, and the investment philosophies of Charles Darwin and Charlie Munger to map the cognitive biases and mental shortcuts that lead to poor decisions. Peter Bevelin uses this multidisciplinary framework to offer practical tools for clearer thinking, drawing on a wide range of thinkers from Aristotle and Montaigne to Feynman and Buffett.

sources.title: Seeking Wisdom: From Darwin To Munger on Amazon

synopsis: Seizing Power develops a coordination-game theory of military coups, arguing that outcomes depend less on firepower than on each faction's ability to signal inevitability and persuade fence-sitters to join the winning side. Drawing on 471 coup attempts from 1950 to 2000 and 300 hours of interviews, Naunihal Singh identifies three structural coup types with distinct probabilities of success.

sources.title: Seizing Power | Johns Hopkins University Press / Project MUSE

synopsis: Semiconductor Device Fundamentals is an undergraduate textbook that covers the theory and operation of core semiconductor devices—including pn junction diodes, bipolar junction transistors, and MOSFETs—building from basic material physics through to practical device analysis. Robert Pierret integrates computer-based exercises throughout to give students hands-on experience modeling device behavior and exploring design trade-offs.

sources.title: Semiconductor Device Fundamentals by Robert F. Pierret on Amazon

synopsis: Serious Cryptography covers the mathematical foundations and practical implementation of modern encryption, including symmetric ciphers, hash functions, public-key cryptography, and protocols such as TLS, with attention to where real-world systems fail. Jean-Philippe Aumasson explains attacker models, authenticated encryption, and advanced techniques like zero-knowledge proofs in a way that bridges theoretical rigor and engineering practice.

sources.title: Serious Cryptography | No Starch Press / Penguin Random House
